title: Needs vs wants (and the ads that blur them)
description: Tell needs from wants before the ads decide for you.
summaryPoints: Run the un-blur test: survivable without it = want
Use the 24-hour rule to outlast impulse buys
When a budget doesn't fit, cut wants — never needs or savings
$60 saved a month is $720 a year of options

content.title: The backpack problem
content.body:
Money is a backpack with limited space. Stuff it with shiny rocks — impulse buys, upgrades you didn't need — and there's no room left for water and food: the stuff that actually gets you through the trip.

Spending wisely isn't "never buy fun things." It's packing on purpose: essentials first, then the extras that genuinely make the trip better. Every dollar you spend is a choice about what no longer fits.

content.title: Needs vs wants
content.body:
**Needs** keep you alive, healthy, and functioning:
- Groceries (not nightly takeout)
- Rent and utilities
- Transport to school or work
- Basic weather-right clothes
- Healthcare and medicine

**Wants** make life nicer, but you'd survive without them: streaming, gaming, designer anything, the new phone when yours works fine.

The line moves with context: a winter coat when yours ripped is a need. A third pair of sneakers is not.
content.imageAlt: A two-pan balance scale weighing needs against wants

content.prompt: Need or want? Some of these are sneaky.

items.text: Bus card to get to school
Newest phone when yours works fine

content.prompt: Wants are bad, and a smart budgeter cuts them all out.
content.explanation: Wants are the fun part — they're half the reason budgets exist. Trouble only starts when wants crowd out needs and savings.

content.title: Why the line gets blurry
content.body:
Companies spend billions making wants FEEL like needs:
- Ads whisper you "need" the new phone to stay connected (your texts send fine)
- Social media serves nonstop vacations and hauls — FOMO is the product
- Friends all have the same sneakers, and suddenly yours feel "broken"

The un-blur test is one question: can I live without this and stay safe and healthy? If yes, it's a want. Buy it if it fits your budget — just never let it cosplay as a need.

content.instructions: One word ruins the un-blur test. Tap it.
content.sentence: If you can live without it and stay safe and healthy, it's a need.
content.explanation: Survivable-without means want. Wants are allowed — they just don't get to skip the line in front of needs and savings.

content.prompt: What is the ad actually doing?
content.context: An ad says: "Everyone's already on the XPhone 17. Don't get left behind." Your phone works fine.
options: Informing you about a genuine need
Dressing a want up as a need using FOMO
Reminding you to check your budget
content.explanation: "Left behind" is pure social pressure. A working phone means the upgrade is a want — maybe a fine one, but a want.

content.title: The 50/30/20 rule
content.body:
A dead-simple way to split every paycheck:
- **50% → Needs** — rent, food, transport, healthcare, bills
- **30% → Wants** — hobbies, takeout, entertainment
- **20% → Savings** — emergencies, big goals, future you

Earn $600 a month? That's $300 for needs, $180 for wants, $120 for savings.

It's not a law — it's a starting grid you adjust to your life. The point is that wants and savings get planned slots instead of fighting over leftovers.

content.prompt: You earn $600 a month. Under 50/30/20, your savings slot is $___.
Takeout at $10 a time, three times a week. Slide to what that costs over a 4-week month.
content.explanation: $10 × 3 × 4 = $120 a month — on a $600 income, that one habit eats your entire savings slot.

content.title: Zoe's quiet flex
content.body:
Zoe earns $200 a month tutoring. Her split:
- Needs: $80 (bus card, school lunches, phone bill)
- Wants: $60 (eating out, one subscription, movie nights)
- Savings: $60

Nothing dramatic — she still eats out, still streams. But after a year she's sitting on $720, while her friend who spent every dollar on fast food and gaming has $0.

When an emergency hits — or an opportunity — Zoe gets to say yes. That's what savings actually buys: options.

content.prompt: Zoe saves $60 every month. After a full year she has $___.
content.explanation: $60 × 12 = $720 — built from small, boring, repeated wins.

content.title: Taming the want monster
content.body:
Four moves that keep wants in their lane:
- **The 24-hour rule** — want it? Wait a day. The urge usually fades; if it doesn't, buy it guilt-free from your wants budget
- **Track spending for a week** — you can't fix what you can't see
- **Give fun a budget** — a set wants amount each month, spent shamelessly
- **Save up for big wants** — stack toward them monthly instead of swiping a credit card

Want a $600 laptop in 6 months? That's $100 a month. Goals beat impulses when they have a number.

content.context: Limited-edition sneakers, $90. Your thumb is hovering over "buy now."
options: Buy immediately — limited means now or never
Put them on a credit card so it doesn't count yet
Wait a day; if you still want them AND they fit your wants budget, go for it
content.explanation: "Limited edition" is urgency bait. A day later the urge often fades — and if it doesn't, you buy with a plan instead of a pulse.

content.prompt: If your income is $1,000 and your spending is $1,150, you have a budget deficit.
content.explanation: Spending more than you earn = deficit. The fix almost always starts with trimming wants — needs and savings stay protected.

content.prompt: You earn $1,000 this month, but the full wishlist costs $1,170. Cover every need, save at least $100 — then trim the wants ($120 eating out, $100 entertainment, $120 clothes) to fit what's left.
content.explanation: Needs ($730) plus savings ($100) leaves $170 for wants — but the wishlist asked for $340. Balancing means cutting wants, never needs or savings. That's the whole skill.
